artifacts.content:
Hi Mum,

I’m writing from my little desk in the studio on A’Beckett Street. It’s nearly midnight here but I can’t sleep again, so I thought I’d tell you properly what’s been happening instead of just saying “everything is fine” on the Sunday calls.

On 25 April (it was a Thursday here), I had a really bad panic attack in the quiet study area on Level 9 of the Swanston Academic Building. Our COSC2627 lab got cancelled at the last minute again and I just… broke. One minute I was rereading the Canvas announcement, the next I was shaking and couldn’t breathe. A staff member walked me down to the RMIT Counselling Service in Building 8 and I saw a psychologist, Dr. Priya Menon.

She was kind, but she said I might be developing an anxiety disorder related to academic performance and being so far from home. She even wrote a note suggesting I reduce my study load for at least six weeks. I haven’t done anything about it because I’m scared it will affect my visa and I don’t want you and Ba to worry more after everything you’ve sacrificed.

The migraines are still coming, especially after bright labs or long nights coding. Dr. Patel at Swanston Street Medical Centre keeps reminding me to rest, but with work at Laneway Brew Café and assignments it feels impossible. I’m trying to eat better again and not skip meals like during Year 12 at Le Hong Phong.

Please don’t panic. I’m still going to classes, and Sarah and Anish help me a lot with Python. I just needed to tell you the truth. Maybe on Sunday we can talk about whether reducing one course for a while is really that terrible.

Con yêu Mẹ,
Emily

metadata.subject: I need to be honest about uni and my health

artifacts.content:
Migraine / mental health log – updated 2026-05-03

Since Feb 2026:
1) Episode 1 – early March, after first COSC2627 lab chaos.
   - Trigger: last‑minute room change + loud lab.
   - Symptoms: pain 8/10, vomited twice, aura, couldn’t stand bright lights.
   - Action: took sumatriptan (Dr. Patel prescription), slept 6 hours.
   - Missed Sunday morning Laneway Brew shift (~$120 lost wages).

2) Episode 2 – late March, around 20/03, when list comprehensions still made zero sense and I was behind on lab sheets.
   - Similar symptoms (8/10, vomiting x2), again missed Sunday shift (~$120).

3) Episode 3 – today, 03/05, right after John Carter rejected my Assignment 2 extension request even with the medical certificate for chronic migraines.
   - Same pattern: 8/10 pain, vomited twice, took sumatriptan, texted shift supervisor to cancel. Third time missing Sunday morning = about $360 total lost.

Also need to remember: 25/04 panic attack in Level 9 quiet study area, Swanston Academic Building → walked to RMIT Counselling in Building 8, saw Dr. Priya Menon. She suggested maybe anxiety disorder + recommended reducing study load for at least 6 weeks. Still too scared about visa + parents to change enrolment.

Action items:
- Ask Dr. Patel at Swanston Street Medical Centre if three severe episodes in two months is “normal” for me.
- Check if blue‑light glasses + stricter sleep schedule help.
- Consider actually following Dr. Menon’s advice about study load before things get worse.
- Talk to Sarah about being accountability buddy for food + breaks so I don’t slip back like Year 12.

Hi Aunty Hoa,

Sorry I’ve been a bit quiet. I keep meaning to call but by the time I finish a shift at Laneway Brew Café in Hardware Lane I’m basically a zombie.

Uni has been rougher than I expected, mostly because of this Programming Fundamentals subject (COSC2627). Since late March, when the labs started going badly, some old problems have been creeping back. You probably remember how in Year 12 at Le Hong Phong High School I saw the school counsellor about my eating. I thought that was behind me, but between 20 March and 30 April this year I lost about 6 kg – from 50 kg down to 44 kg.

On 28 April I saw Dr. Patel at Swanston Street Medical Centre. He weighed me and was pretty serious. He said if I drop below 43 kg he’ll have to refer me back to specialised eating disorder services at the Royal Melbourne Hospital. Hearing “hospital” again was like being punched. I promised I’d try to stabilise – three real meals a day, not just coffee and leftover toasties from the café.

At the same time, after they refused my extension on Assignment 2 on 6 May, I panicked about failing and having to repeat the course. I picked up more shifts – now about 28 hours a week instead of ~20. I keep thinking about how you told me Ba and Mum took that 250,000,000 VND education loan in late 2023 for my first year at RMIT. The idea of wasting their money makes me feel sick.

I know you’re busy with work in Footscray, but if you have time maybe we could have phở together next weekend? I think just talking in Vietnamese with you face‑to‑face would calm me down.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Can we have lunch sometime soon?

artifacts.content:
Emily: Hey Sarah, you free to meet in Building 80 tomorrow arvo to go over the Week 7 COSC2627 lab? I still don’t fully get the data structures bit.

Sarah: Yeah, I can do 3pm near the Level 7 booths. You okay after today?

Emily: Honestly, not really. After John said “other students manage full-time work and still get HDs” when I asked about balancing my ~20 hours at Laneway Brew, I went straight to the bathroom near lab room 08.09.25 and just cried.

Emily: It brought back those intrusive self-harm thoughts from early 2023. I’m not planning anything, but it scared me how quickly my brain went there.

Sarah: I’m so sorry Em. That comment was brutal. Do you want me to come over tonight?

Emily: Maybe not tonight. I’m exhausted from crying. But can we talk properly tomorrow? I even started wondering if staying in Melbourne away from my family is worth it if I fail this core course.

Emily: A couple of weeks ago (25/04) I had a full-on panic attack in the Level 9 quiet study area in the Swanston building. They took me to RMIT Counselling in Building 8 and I saw a psychologist, Dr. Priya Menon. She said it might be an anxiety disorder and suggested reducing my study load for 6 weeks.

Emily: I didn’t tell my parents everything because of the visa and money stuff. I feel stuck.

Sarah: Tomorrow we’ll sit down, do the lab, and also look at options, okay? You’re not alone.

Emily: Thanks. 3pm tomorrow, Level 7 booths. I’ll bring snacks so I actually eat something.

Budget + work hours reflection – 2026-05-10

Context: After Assignment 2 extension request was denied on 03/05, I freaked out about possibly failing COSC2627 and needing to repeat it (international fees again = nightmare). On 06/05 I asked my manager at Laneway Brew Café if I could pick up more shifts. Now doing ~28 hours/week instead of ~20.

Money side:
- Extra ~8 hrs/week at ~$22/hr before tax ≈ $176/week.
- Part of me feels better knowing I’m saving in case I have to pay for the course again.
- But I keep thinking about Ba and Mum still paying off that 250,000,000 VND education loan they took in late 2023 for my first year at RMIT. I don’t want them to know how close I feel to failing.

Health side:
- More shifts = more time on my feet, bright café lights, noise → not great for migraines.
- Three major migraine episodes since Feb 2026 already, each 8/10 pain with vomiting twice, needing sumatriptan and missing a Sunday morning shift (~$120 lost each time). Extra hours might just cancel themselves out if I keep getting sick.
- Weight: 44 kg at last appointment with Dr. Patel on 28/04, down from 50 kg on 20/03. If I fall below 43 kg he said he has to refer me to eating disorder services at Royal Melbourne Hospital. Terrifying.

Plan:
- Cap at 25 hrs/week unless absolutely desperate.
- Prioritise food + sleep over extra money (otherwise hospital instead of uni).
- Re-read Dr. Priya Menon’s advice about possibly reducing study load for 6 weeks.
- Talk to David about realistic budget so I’m not just guessing.

Event: Follow-up with Dr. Patel – migraines + weight
Date: Tuesday, 2026-04-28
Time: 3:15–4:00 pm
Location: Swanston Street Medical Centre (GP clinic)

Details / things to discuss:
- Weight loss since semester started: from ~50 kg on 20/03 to 44 kg now (lost about 6 kg). Ask if the stress from COSC2627 could really trigger my old disordered eating patterns from Year 12 at Le Hong Phong again.
- Dr. Patel warned that if my weight drops below 43 kg he’ll need to refer me to the eating disorder services at Royal Melbourne Hospital. Need to ask what that would actually involve (inpatient? day program?) and how it might affect my study and visa.
- Update him on three severe migraine episodes since Feb 2026, all linked to academic stress at RMIT (especially the Assignment 2 extension rejection on 03/05 – mention even though it’s a future date from this appointment, pattern is clear already). Symptoms each time: pain level 8/10, vomiting twice, needed sumatriptan, missed Sunday morning Laneway Brew shift (~$120 lost wages per episode).
- Mention panic attack on 25/04 in Level 9 quiet study area, Swanston Academic Building, and that RMIT psychologist Dr. Priya Menon at Counselling in Building 8 suggested I might have an anxiety disorder and recommended reducing study load for at least 6 weeks.
- Ask whether he thinks a reduced load is medically necessary and how to balance that with financial pressure (parents’ 250,000,000 VND education loan from late 2023).

Hi Ba,

I know you’re busy with work in Ho Chi Minh City, but I wanted to explain something properly instead of just saying “uni is stressful” on our Sunday video calls.

This programming subject, COSC2627, has been much harder than I expected. When my extension request for Assignment 2 was rejected on 03/05, even though I sent a medical certificate for my migraines from Dr. Patel, I started to panic about failing. It’s a core course, so if I don’t pass I might have to repeat it next year and pay the full international fee again.

Because of that, on 06/05 I asked my manager at Laneway Brew Café if I could take more shifts. I’ve gone from about 20 hours a week to around 28. I’m trying to save extra money in case I need it for fees or if my health gets worse and I can’t work later in the semester.

I keep thinking about the 250,000,000 VND education loan you and Mum took in late 2023 to cover my first year at RMIT. I feel guilty every time I think about that number. I don’t want you to worry, but I also don’t want to hide things from you.

The good news is that I’m not alone here. Aunty Hoa checks in on me, and David sometimes meets me near Melbourne Central for bubble tea when I look too stressed. I also saw a psychologist at RMIT Counselling in Building 8, Dr. Priya Menon, after a panic attack in the Swanston Academic Building on 25/04. She thinks I might need to slow down a bit.

I’m working hard, Ba. I just wanted you to know what’s really going on.

Con,
Emily
